notable.notable_blurb: Designer of Central Park; co-founded The Nation

cross_connections.display_label: Asked Godkin to found The Nation in 1865
Central Park commissioner alongside him from 1857
Consulted Olcott on Central Park and Bushnell Park turf

institutions.institution_blurb: His firm designed the Retreat's grounds — first CT commission.

narrative_blocks.header: Designed Central Park; founder of American landscape architecture
narrative_blocks.body: His winning 1858 'Greensward Plan' with Calvert Vaux launched the discipline of American landscape architecture. Before that career he traveled the antebellum South for The New-York Daily Times and wrote 'The Cotton Kingdom' (1861), a meticulous account of slavery's brutality that helped keep British opinion against the Confederacy. Buffalo's first urban park system, Boston's Emerald Necklace, the US Capitol grounds, and the Biltmore Estate followed his Central Park work.

narrative_blocks.header: Charles Wyllys Elliott was his Central Park commissioner
narrative_blocks.body: Elliott — the homeware designer and Wyllys-Hooker descendant — served as one of the original commissioners overseeing the construction of Central Park beginning in 1857. The two worked together on the project's first decade of design and execution.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded The Nation with Edwin Lawrence Godkin
narrative_blocks.body: He led the abolitionist circle that asked the Irish-born journalist Godkin to start The Nation magazine in 1865, contributing initial financing and editorial direction. Godkin married Frances Elizabeth Foote, a Hooker-line descendant from New Haven, becoming a long-standing member of the family's Connecticut social circle.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in Hartford, the Hooker family's founding city
narrative_blocks.body: Hartford was settled in 1636 by the Rev. Thomas Hooker, whose descendants populated the city for the two centuries before Olmsted's 1822 birth. He later returned to design the grounds of the Hartford Retreat — institution and city both threaded through Hooker family history.

INST079.name: Hartford Retreat for the Insane
INST079.notes:
Founded 1822, built 1823, opened to patients 1824. One of four such facilities in the nation at founding. First superintendent: Dr. Eli Todd (1823-1833) — pioneered "moral care" of the insane. T01062 Phineas Talcott Jr. served as steward 1828-1835; his wife I00197 Lora McLean was matron — patients called her "Good Mother Talcott." Boasted highest cure rate in the nation in its first decade. Grounds landscaped by Frederick Law Olmsted, 1860s. Became Institute of Living in 1943; incorporated into Hartford Hospital 1994. Now a comprehensive psychiatric facility at 200 Retreat Avenue, Hartford. Connecticut History Online article covers history in detail. [Apr 2026 cleanup — merged from duplicate INST108]: Chartered by Connecticut General Assembly 1822 following petition from the Connecticut State Medical Society; opened April 4, 1824. The only mental asylum in the United States founded by a state medical society. Eli Todd took up the cause after his sister's depression and suicide. Sources: APA Foundation; Goodheart 'Mad Yankees' (UMass Press, 2003); Braceland 'Institute of Living: The Hartford Retreat 1822-1972'; Connecticut History (CTHumanities).
[former/alt detail] Now the Institute of Living (Hartford Hospital campus)
